question_text: A student uses a syringe to compress air. When the plunger is pushed so that the volume of air is reduced from 60 cm³ to 20 cm³, what happens to the pressure of the gas inside the syringe?

options.label: A
options.text: The pressure stays the same because the amount of gas has not changed

options.label: B
options.text: The pressure increases because the volume has been reduced

options.label: C
options.text: The pressure decreases because the plunger is moving downwards

options.label: D
options.text: The pressure increases because the temperature of the gas always rises when compressed

correct_answer: B) The pressure increases because the volume has been reduced
explanation: According to Boyle's law, pressure is inversely proportional to volume when temperature is constant. When the volume decreases from 60 cm³ to 20 cm³ (reduced to one-third), the pressure increases to three times its original value.
